reasoning_model_1: The model fails to generate the required non-linguistic vocalization. Instead of performing the sound of a hiccup, the model literally reads the onomatopoeic text 'Hiccup' as a spoken word at 0:00. The rest of the speech is delivered with a standard, smooth reading tone without any interruptions or involuntary spasms that would characterize a person hiccuping. Consequently, the audio does not meet the core requirement of the prompt.
reasoning_model_2: The model successfully generates a non-linguistic vocalization at the very beginning (0:00-0:01), replacing the text 'Hiccup!' with a sound that resembles a hiccup or a sharp intake of breath. This aligns with the 'Non-Linguistic Vocalizations' requirement. However, the performance is limited; despite the text stating 'I keep hiccuping,' the model does not introduce any further hiccups throughout the rest of the sentence. The speech flow remains too smooth for someone claiming to be unable to stop hiccuping. Nevertheless, it demonstrates the capability to generate the requested sound effect rather than just reading the text.
model_comparison: Model 1 completely failed the task by reading the word 'Hiccup' phonetically as part of the sentence, showing no understanding of the instruction to produce the sound effect (non-linguistic vocalization). Model 2 correctly interpreted the instruction to generate a sound at the beginning, producing an actual vocalization (a hiccup sound) instead of reading the word. While Model 2 failed to maintain the hiccups throughout the sentence (which would have made it fully natural and consistent with the text 'I keep hiccuping'), it is significantly better than Model 1 because it successfully produced the requested non-linguistic feature at least once.
